items.q: How do greenhouse gases warm Earth?
items.correct: They absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate some back down.
items.misconception: They block heat from escaping into space (like a literal lid).
items.random_wrong: They reflect sunlight back to Earth.
items.why: Students picture a physical blanket. The mechanism is selective absorption - greenhouse gases let visible light pass IN but absorb outgoing infrared. The re-emission warms the lower atmosphere and surface.

items.q: Why is ice-albedo feedback a positive feedback loop?
items.correct: Less ice means less reflective surface, so more sunlight is absorbed, causing more warming and more melting.
items.misconception: Because warming is positive (a good thing).
items.random_wrong: Because polar bears help spread heat.
items.why: Students confuse 'positive' (mathematics) with 'good' (everyday). Positive feedback amplifies the change, in any direction - a negative outcome can result from a positive feedback loop.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: Which gas has the highest 100-yr GWP per kg of those listed?
items.why: It's HFC-23 (~14 800). “CH₄ (~25)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: W m⁻². “ppm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If 200 kg of CH₄ leak from a pipeline (GWP=25), the CO₂-equivalent emissions (kg) are?
items.why: The real figure is 5000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
